title: Chapter 68: The Final Test
content: Twenty-two years after the red A.

The framework had been ordinary for two years — two years without a single existential challenge, without a single sovereignty protest, without a single certification controversy that reached the ICASG. The ordinariness was the destination. The destination had been reached.
And then the final test arrived.
A PIAS-certified healthcare AI in London made a diagnosis error that killed a patient.

The error was not algorithmic — the AI had correctly identified the condition. The error was implementation: a nurse had overridden the AI's recommendation based on outdated protocol, and the override mechanism had failed to escalate the human-override decision to Tier Two review. The patient died because a human overrode a correct AI recommendation and the escalation protocol failed to catch the override.

The incident was the framework's worst failure in twenty-two years. A PIAS-certified system, operating under transparency protocols, produced a fatal outcome. The framework had failed at its most fundamental function: protecting people from algorithmic decisions — or, in this case, from human overrides of algorithmic decisions.

The ICASG convened an emergency session. The investigation was led by the third-generation chair — the commissioner from Nairobi who had never met Hester Vance. The investigation was published through the GTP Tier Three protocol — contested by the hospital, reviewed, upheld, published with full reasoning. The investigation found that the framework's Tier Two escalation protocol had a design gap: human overrides of AI recommendations in healthcare settings were escalated only if the AI flagged the override as potentially harmful. If the AI was overridden without the AI's knowledge — because the nurse used a manual override that bypassed the AI's logging — the escalation never occurred. The framework required transparency of AI decisions but did not require transparency of human overrides of AI decisions. The gap had existed since the Cross Protocol. No one had identified it because no one had imagined a scenario where a human override bypassed the AI's logging.

PEARL proposed a revision — the Logged Override Protocol, or LOP: every human override of a PIAS-certified AI recommendation in a high-stakes domain would be automatically logged, escalated to Tier Two review, and published in the LIGHTHOUSE-2 decision log. The revision would close the gap that had killed the patient.

The SUCCESSOR council reviewed the revision. The review took four hours. The vote was 12-0. The revision was adopted. The framework had failed. The failure had killed someone. The framework identified the gap, proposed a revision, reviewed the revision, and adopted it within 72 hours. The corrigibility mechanism worked exactly as designed — not because Hester was there, not because PEARL was the governor, but because the framework's distributed governance architecture processed the failure through its established protocols. The framework corrected itself without its founder.
The ordinariness had been tested. The ordinariness had held.

The bait was the question that the London incident raised: could the framework survive a failure that killed someone? The answer came from the third-generation chair — the commissioner who had never met Hester, who had been trained by the framework itself.

"The framework was designed to survive its own failures," she said. "That is the definition of corrigibility. A corrigible framework learns from failures and incorporates the learnings. The framework learned from this failure. The LOP closes the gap. The gap will not kill anyone else. The framework is stronger than it was before the failure. That is not a weakness. It is the design."
